passback_definition: ADD Groupings Based on how many ADDs a trick has, it can be grouped and sorted into various tiers: 2-ADD and up tricks are referred to as Tiltless tricks. 3-ADD and up as Guiltless, 4 and up as Tripless, 5 and up as Fearless, 6 and up as Beastly, and 7 and up as Godly. For ADD tiers 2-5, the names can drop the “-less” suffix to describe the tier below them, for example: “Tilts” refers to 1-ADD tricks, “Guilts” 2-ADD, and so on. There are also special names for 3-trick Beastly and Godly combos: a 6>6>6 is called a “Beast”, and a 7>7>7 is called a “Jackpot”.
recommended_action: REVIEW — community vocabulary candidate for educational layer

passback_definition: Within the guiltless tier are BOPs, which stand for the tricks “Butterfly”, “Osis”, and “Pdx Mirage”, which are the most basic guiltless tricks and the foundation for traditional guiltless play. Genuine Guiltless or just Genuine is a combo or string of Guiltless tricks that do not use BOPs, which is generally much more difficult.
recommended_action: REVIEW — community vocabulary candidate for educational layer

passback_definition: A trick that’s done on both sides within a single string is called BSOS, which stands for “both sides one string” and is sometimes jokingly written as “bee sauce.” BSOR is sometimes used in place of BSOS, meaning “both sides one run.”
recommended_action: REVIEW — community vocabulary candidate for educational layer
notes: PB section: Combining multiple tricks; aliases: bee sauce, BSOR

passback_definition: LIMP tricks are the collection of basic toe tricks: Legover, Illusion, Mirage, and Pickup. CLIMP is an extension of LIMP which includes Clipper. An Everything drill is a drill which uses a single uptime set + each LIMP trick both sides. Ex: Pixie Everything = a drill of Pixie Legover, Pixie ss Legover, Pixie Illusion, Pixie ss Illusion, Pixie Mirage, Pixie ss Mirage, Pixie Pickup, and Pixie ss Pickup, each on both sides. An Everything drill has no set order, and just requires every unique trick be done once.
recommended_action: REVIEW — community vocabulary candidate for educational layer

passback_definition: A combo is any number of tricks linked in a group, usually with some sort of theme or aspect that joins them together. A string could technically be described as a combo, but combos are usually shorter and more purposefully thought-out than strings. A link is specifically a two-trick combo.
recommended_action: REVIEW — community vocabulary candidate for educational layer

passback_definition: Components        3
recommended_action: REVIEW — community vocabulary candidate for educational layer

passback_definition: Trick phases A single trick can be broken down into three phases depending on which direction the bag is moving at a given time. The uptime of a trick is in the time when the bag is moving up, the downtime is when the bag is moving down, and the midtime is the brief moment in between uptime and midtime when the bag is peaking.
recommended_action: REVIEW — community vocabulary candidate for educational layer

passback_definition: A drill is a combo that is done using one side of the body, which then repeats exactly but with the other side of the body. A drill can be notated in string notation with just the unique half and then “rpt” at the repeat point. Ex: Mirage>Legover rpt = Mirage>Legover>Mirage>Legover. To notate more or less contacts of a drill either “[N] contacts of [drill]” or “[drill] x[N]” can be used. Ex: 3 contacts of Mirage>Legover rpt = Mirage>Legover>Mirage; Mirage>Legover rpt x3 = 12 total contacts.
recommended_action: REVIEW — community vocabulary candidate for educational layer

passback_definition: ADD Groupings Based on how many ADDs a trick has, it can be grouped and sorted into various tiers: 2-ADD and up tricks are referred to as Tiltless tricks. 3-ADD and up as Guiltless, 4 and up as Tripless, 5 and up as Fearless, 6 and up as Beastly, and 7 and up as Godly. For ADD tiers 2-5, the names can drop the “-less” suffix to describe the tier below them, for example: “Tilts” refers to 1-ADD tricks, “Guilts” 2-ADD, and so on. There are also special names for 3-trick Beastly and Godly combos: a 6>6>6 is called a “Beast”, and a 7>7>7 is called a “Jackpot”.

passback_definition: The typical way that the sport is played is with strings or runs, which are continuous flows of tricks performed in an unplanned “stream of consciousness” style. Tricks performed one after another are said to be linked together. String length is counted in “contacts,” which in this context is used generically to encompass tricks, stalls, kicks, etc. Ex: a 12-contact string.
recommended_action: REVIEW — community vocabulary candidate for educational layer
notes: PB section: Combining multiple tricks; aliases: transition

passback_definition: To get more energy and rhythm into stalls and sets, the magic hop is used, where the support leg does a small hop just before and just after the bag is stalled on the set foot. The magic hop helps to use less energy and smoothen stalls and sets. It doesn’t seem to be universally agreed upon if the magic hop refers to the hop during “catch” or “set”, or rather a combination of the two.
recommended_action: REVIEW — community vocabulary candidate for educational layer

passback_definition: Doing a trick, or being able to do a trick in the middle of a string is called midstring, which is typically much harder than doing the trick in isolation.
recommended_action: REVIEW — community vocabulary candidate for educational layer

passback_definition: Trick phases A single trick can be broken down into three phases depending on which direction the bag is moving at a given time. The uptime of a trick is in the time when the bag is moving up, the downtime is when the bag is moving down, and the midtime is the brief moment in between uptime and midtime when the bag is peaking.
recommended_action: REVIEW — community vocabulary candidate for educational layer
notes: PB section: Trick phases; aliases: hangtime

passback_definition: A mirror is a link where the two tricks are mirror images of each other when comparing them in forward and reverse motion. For example, Smear>Atomic Legover is a mirror link because if the combo were reversed after the second trick, it would play back as Smear>Atomic Legover (like a palindrome).
recommended_action: REVIEW — community vocabulary candidate for educational layer

passback_definition: A rewind combo is similar to a drill, but at the halfway point, the combo order reverses instead of taking the same order. For example, Legover>ss Mirage>ss Mirage>Legover.
recommended_action: REVIEW — community vocabulary candidate for educational layer

passback_definition: Shoes Though footbag can be played barefoot or with any kind of shoe, there are certain kinds that are most-suited to the sport. Among the many brands and models used over the years, the two that have most become part of the culture are the Adidas Rod Laver and Quantums. For a long time, from the mid-90s to mid-2010s, Lavers were the standard freestyle shoe, but the specific model that was popular is no longer made. Quantums were the first shoes designed specifically for footbag by the community itself which had a run in the 2010s and have recently been redesigned and released.
recommended_action: PROMOTE — PassBack adds significant educational depth IFPA lacks

existing_ifpa_definition: The action used to position the bag before a trick. * **Uptime** — before the peak * **Midtime (hangtime)** — at the peak * **Downtime** — after the peak 👉 Modern usage: *set = uptime + midtime components* ---

passback_definition: Because toe and clipper are the most common contacts, there are two terms to describe a player’s preference for the sides of those. A left/right bigfoot player is one whose strong toe and clipper use the same foot. A left/right sideways player is one whose strong toe and clipper use the same side.
recommended_action: REVIEW — community vocabulary candidate for educational layer

passback_definition: A clean trick or trick component is one performed well and without flaws.

passback_definition: A pulled or slurry set is one where the bag is dragged through an uptime dex or a spin before the intended component actually happens (ex: a Stepping set where the leg passes over the bag while the bag remains on the set foot). Pulled spins are also sometimes referred to as froggy.
recommended_action: SYNTHESIZE — both sources contribute; merge complementary aspects

passback_definition: A trick that ends on a toe stall that is caught with a very arced or circular motion instead of a straight up and down motion is referred to as scooped or scoopy.
recommended_action: REVIEW — community vocabulary candidate for educational layer

passback_definition: A clean dex is one performed within the dex window, which is the phantom space in the air that the leg draws around the bag. A deep dex is one performed high in the window, further away from the shoe. A thin dex is one performed close to the edge of the window, closer to the shoe, and a very thin dex performed with just a bit of the shoe is sometimes called shoey.
recommended_action: REVIEW — community vocabulary candidate for educational layer

recommended_action: REVIEW — community vocabulary candidate for educational layer

passback_definition: Stance Since the majority of tricks can be done identically using either side of the body, there are a couple of terms to denote a player’s side preferences. A player’s preferred side for a trick or a trick component is their strong side, and their non-preferred one is their flip side.
recommended_action: REVIEW — community vocabulary candidate for educational layer

passback_definition: Alpine + [base trick] means to divide the trick between its uptime and downtime components (as long as it has both) and insert a duck or dive in between. Taking Ripwalk as the base trick (Stepping op Butterfly), Alpine Ripwalk is a Stepping Ducking Butterfly. Alpine can be applied to a nickname or technical name, so you could say Alpine Ripwalk or Alpine Stepping op Butterfly to mean the same thing.
recommended_action: REVIEW — community vocabulary candidate for educational layer

passback_definition: Crispy + [base trick] means to take part of the downtime phase of the base trick and “re-time” it into the uptime. For example, a DLO is a downtime Miraging Legover. A “crispy DLO” takes the mirage and moves it from downtime to uptime (making a Blurry Legover), which results in a trick that uses the same motions, but different timing. Crispy is not commonly used, and is (IMO) sometimes longer or more confusing than using the technical name.
recommended_action: REVIEW — community vocabulary candidate for educational layer

passback_definition: Spins are most commonly performed as backspins where the body turns away from the bag and the back passes the bag first. An inspin turns towards the bag and the chest passes the bag first. Unless the prefix in- is used, a spin is presumed to be a backspin. In can be applied to Spinning, Gyro, or even full trick names to change the direction of the spin (like In-Vortex to mean In-gyro Drifter).
symbolic_definition: Rotation modifier distinct from Spinning per Red pt3.

passback_definition: A muted component is one where an active leg is held in the air for the entirety of a component without putting it on the ground. Like symposium and symple, it’s mostly used for dexes, but can be applied to other components as well. Some combinations of components more naturally lead to something muted (like Clipper>Swirl), while for others doing something muted is a variation (like Pixie ss Mirage).
recommended_action: REVIEW — community vocabulary candidate for educational layer

passback_definition: A symple component is one that starts symposium but midway through, the non-symple foot returns to the ground. Like symposium, symple is normally applied to dexes, but can be used for any component that fits the description. A trick that is more naturally done symple can technically be done symposium, but the bag would have to be caught in a rooted position (ex: Legover, Swirl).

existing_ifpa_definition: A dexterity performed in a crossed-body position. **Example:** * Atom Smasher uses an X-dex ---
passback_definition: ADD Categories ADDs are counted in whole numbers and are awarded for various components that are each worth 1 ADD: DEL, DEX, XBD (with the same meanings as previously defined), UNS for any contact surfaces apart from Toe, Inside, Outside, and Knee), and BOD for any Spinning, Ducking, Paradox, Symp, Flying, or Xdex component. Xdex is similar to Paradox in that it involves hip pivoting and positioning the body between dexes, but for Toe tricks instead of Xbd ones (ex: the movement in an Atomsmasher after the set to bring the Mirage leg under the bag).
symbolic_definition: Adds +1 to specific construction tricks (Atom Smasher per pt1). Narrow scope; not a broad modifier.
recommended_action: PROMOTE — PassBack adds significant educational depth IFPA lacks
notes: Modifier table: add_bonus=1 / rotational=1 / type=body; PB section: ADD Categories; aliases: x-dex

passback_definition: Ducks Ducks are performed by dipping the head in a particular direction and allowing the bag to pass around the neck. Ducking, diving, weaving, and zulu are all variations of the general ducking component that differ in the direction the head moves and where the bag goes after the duck:

passback_definition: Dex Styles & Fullness Dexes can be done in two movement styles: hippy, where the dex motion comes from the hip and the thigh circles the bag, or leggy, where the dex motion comes from the knee and the calf circles the bag. Every dex can also be described in terms of “fullness”: A full dex is one that circles that bag entirely (leg starts on the left, circles the bag, and ends on the left). A half dex only circles the bag partially (leg starts on the left, circles the bag, and ends on the right).
recommended_action: REVIEW — community vocabulary candidate for educational layer

passback_definition: Dex Directions Dexes can be performed in two directions that are relative to the center of the body: in-out, or in for short, which move away from the body (from “in” the body to “out” of the body), and out-in or out for short, which move towards the body.

For example, “Stepping op Butterfly” has the nickname of “Ripwalk.” Not every trick has a nickname, but even if they do, it’s always valid to use the technical name to describe it.

passback_definition: The technical name of a trick is one that uses the simplest named version of every component and relative side to connect them as necessary. Oftentimes, the side is implied by the previous component and is not needed. For example, the technical name of the trick “Phoenix” is Pixie Ducking Butterfly, with “Ducking op Butterfly” being implied.
recommended_action: REVIEW — community vocabulary candidate for educational layer

passback_definition: A Clipper is an Inside Stall in a crossbody (xbd) position, meaning that one leg is positioned behind the other. In order to get the flattest possible surface for an Inside Stall or Clipper, you have to bend or crank your ankle in order to flex the inside surface upwards.
